originalTitle: This farmer wanted to quit the cocaine industry - he couldn't
summary: Colombians who grow cocaine's raw material bemoan a lack of support to help them switch crops.
content:
Coca farmer Perea says he has not had the financial support required to stop growing the crop

When Perea stopped growing coca - the raw material used to make cocaine - he vowed never to plant it again.

He uprooted the green bushes on his small farm in a remote corner of the province of Meta in Colombia, reachable only by river, and replaced them with legal crops like cassava and plantain.

Perea was one of thousands who joined a government-run crop substitution programme aimed at helping farmers abandon coca.

But much of the promised assistance never arrived, and a lack of roads and recurring floods made it difficult to sell his produce.

In 2024, disillusioned, he began planting coca again.

"It's a tragedy," says Perea. "But when you have children and no work, what choice do you have? If no help ever arrives, you go back to growing it."

Colombia's escalating, brutal internal conflict is defining its presidential election

BBC joins Colombian commandos fighting 'never-ending battle' against drug gangs

The coca leaf is an ancestral crop traditionally used by indigenous communities in teas and medicines. But today most of it is processed into cocaine. An estimated 70% of the global supply of the illegal drug comes from Colombia.

"Coca has some major advantages over other crops," says Lucas Marín Llanes, a Colombian researcher who focuses on the coca economy and substitution strategies. "Harvests are quick - farmers can get three or four a year - it's easier to transport, and farmers know what price they'll get."

Research Marín has worked on shows coca cultivation can also boost local economies, increasing municipal GDP by as much as 10% in some areas between 2014 and 2019.

Substitution programmes were designed to help Colombian coca farmers move away from the crop and build legal livelihoods.

Yet today the planting of coca is at record levels at more than 250,000 hectares - and many Colombians in substitution programmes say they have been let down.

Coca leaves, which can be processed into the drug cocaine

Elena Hernández moved to the coca-growing region of Guaviare during the boom of the 1990s, lured by better pay.

"There was more money back then - you could see it everywhere," she says. "I managed to save and buy a small house."

But the coca industry also brought violence and insecurity. Armed groups fought for control, while the government tried to curb production through manual eradication, aerial fumigation and arrests.

These efforts cut off families' incomes but failed to stem cultivation.

Each household was due to get 36m Colombian pesos ($11,000; £8,000), spread over a two-year period.

"The way it was presented to us, it seemed very promising for the development of our territory," says Hernández.

The programme, known as the National Comprehensive Programme for the Substitution of Illicit Crops (PNIS), was born out of the 2016 peace deal between the government and Colombia's largest guerrilla group, Farc.

In exchange for uprooting their coca plants, farmers would also get technical support, including from agronomists, and advice on soil management.

While substitution programmes had existed before, they remained limited in scale. PNIS was the most ambitious attempt to date - and at first appeared to be working.

In parts of southern Meta and Guaviare, coca fields were replaced by lemon trees, banana crops and small livestock farms. Many farmers, like Hernández, stayed away from coca.

Yet that does not mean they consider it a success.

"The government wasn't very committed to us farmers. We were treated badly," explains Hernández, pointing to problems that emerged early on.

Payments were delayed, and technical support frequently failed to materialise. Weak state presence in rural areas and poor coordination between agencies meant support often did not reach communities.

Momentum behind the programme weakened as political priorities shifted. Iván Duque, who became Colombia's president in 2018, refocused the government's approach on eradication and security strategies.

By the time current President Gustavo Petro took office in 2022, PNIS was behind schedule and struggling to reach communities.

But some farmers did report improvements. For Doralba Bejarano, from Puerto Rico in southern Meta, her situation improved as stalled support began to arrive.

"Before the programme we were terrified," she says. "We had to hide the coca paste because the police, the military, would put us in jail." Coca paste is a more concentrated form of coca used to produce cocaine, allowing growers to earn more than selling raw leaves.

Now, she says, she has peace of mind: "I go wherever I want. I have no reason to hide."

She says many in her community are now receiving government support to promote and sell their non-coca crops and other foodstuffs.

Doralba Bejarano says she has peace of mind after giving up growing coca leaves for the cocaine industry.

The challenges facing substitution are also driven by forces beyond Colombia's borders – continuing huge appetite for cocaine in the US and Europe, as well as newer markets elsewhere.

"Right now there is expanding demand, and so there will be supply to meet that," says Michael Weintraub, co-director of the Centre for the Study of Security and Drugs at the University of the Andes, which is based in Colombia's capital Bogota. "That means Colombia will grow coca for the foreseeable future."

Weintraub also describes the security context in Colombia as "fragile" - making it difficult for substitution programmes to operate effectively in many rural areas.

Although the 2016 peace deal led to the disbanding of large parts of Farc, other armed groups have moved in to fill the vacuum, and often control trafficking routes and the local economies.

The current government has sought to address this through its "Total Peace" policy, by which it holds peace negotiations with all the various armed and criminal gangs. But analysts say progress has
chineseTitle: 这位农民想退出可卡因行业 - 但他做不到
chineseSummary: 种植可卡因原料的哥伦比亚人哀叹缺乏帮助他们转变作物的支持。
chineseContent:
古柯种植者佩雷亚表示，他没有获得停止种植古柯作物所需的财政支持

当佩雷亚停止种植古柯（用于制造可卡因的原材料）时，他发誓不再种植古柯。

他把位于哥伦比亚梅塔省偏远角落的小农场里的绿色灌木连根拔起，只能通过河流到达，并用木薯和车前草等合法作物取而代之。

佩雷亚是参加政府开展的农作物替代计划的数千人之一，该计划旨在帮助农民放弃古柯。

但许多承诺的援助从未到达，而且缺乏道路和反复发生的洪水使他的产品很难出售。

2024年，幻想破灭的他再次开始种植古柯。

“这是一场悲剧，”佩雷亚说。 “但是当你有了孩子却没有工作时，你有什么选择呢？如果没有帮助到来，你就回去种植它。”

哥伦比亚不断升级的残酷内部冲突正在决定其总统选举

BBC 加入哥伦比亚突击队，与贩毒团伙进行“永无休止的战斗”

古柯叶是土著社区传统上用于茶和药物的祖传作物。但如今大部分都被加工成可卡因。据估计，全球非法毒品供应的 70% 来自哥伦比亚。

“与其他作物相比，古柯有一些主要优势，”专注于古柯经济和替代战略的哥伦比亚研究人员卢卡斯·马林·利亚内斯 (Lucas Marín Llanes) 说。 “收成很快——农民一年可以收获三到四个——运输更方便，农民知道他们会得到什么价格。”

Marín 所做的研究表明，古柯种植还可以促进当地经济，在 2014 年至 2019 年间，某些地区的城市 GDP 增加了 10%。

替代计划旨在帮助哥伦比亚古柯种植者摆脱这种作物并建立合法的生计。

然而如今，古柯种植面积达到创纪录水平，超过 25 万公顷，许多参与替代计划的哥伦比亚人表示，他们感到失望。

古柯叶，可加工成毒品可卡因

在 20 世纪 90 年代经济繁荣时期，埃琳娜·埃尔南德斯 (Elena Hernández) 受到更高工资的诱惑，搬到了古柯种植区瓜维亚雷 (Guaviare)。

“那时有更多的钱——你随处都可以看到它，”她说。 “我设法存钱买了一套小房子。”

但古柯产业也带来了暴力和不安全感。武装团体为控制权而战，而政府则试图通过人工铲除、空中熏蒸和逮捕来限制生产。

这些努力切断了家庭的收入，但未能阻止种植。

每户家庭将在两年内获得 3600 万哥伦比亚比索（11,000 美元；8,000 英镑）。

“从向我们展示的方式来看，它似乎对我们领土的发展很有希望，”埃尔南德斯说。

该计划被称为国家非法作物替代综合计划 (PNIS)，诞生于 2016 年政府与哥伦比亚最大游击队哥伦比亚革命武装力量 (Farc) 之间达成的和平协议。

作为连根拔起古柯植物的交换，农民还将获得技术支持，包括农艺师的支持，以及土壤管理的建议。

虽然替代计划以前就已经存在，但规模仍然有限。 PNIS 是迄今为止最雄心勃勃的尝试，而且一开始似乎正在发挥作用。

在梅塔和瓜维亚雷南部的部分地区，古柯田被柠檬树、香蕉作物和小型畜牧场所取代。许多农民，比如埃尔南德斯，都远离古柯。

但这并不意味着他们认为这是成功的。

埃尔南德斯解释说：“政府对我们农民并不是很忠诚。我们受到了恶劣的对待。”他指出了早期出现的问题。

付款被延迟，技术支持也经常无法兑现。国家在农村地区的影响力薄弱，机构之间协调不力，意味着支持往往无法到达社区。

随着政治优先事项的转变，该计划背后的动力减弱。 2018 年就任哥伦比亚总统的伊万·杜克将政府的方针重新集中在根除和安全战略上。

到 2022 年现任总统古斯塔沃·佩特罗 (Gustavo Petro) 上任时，PNIS 已落后于计划并难以到达社区。

但一些农民确实报告了情况有所改善。对于来自波多黎各梅塔南部的多拉尔巴·贝哈拉诺来说，随着停滞不前的支持开始到来，她的处境有所改善。

“在这个项目之前我们很害怕，”她说。 “我们不得不隐藏古柯酱，因为警察、军队会把我们关进监狱。”古柯糊是一种更浓缩的古柯形式，用于生产可卡因，使种植者比销售生叶赚得更多。

现在，她说，她安心了：“我想去哪儿就去哪儿。我没有理由躲起来。”

她说，她所在社区的许多人现在正在接受政府的支持，以推广和销售他们的非古柯作物和其他食品。

多拉尔巴·贝哈拉诺 (Doralba Bejarano) 表示，放弃种植古柯叶转而从事可卡因行业后，她感到安心。

替代品面临的挑战也是由哥伦比亚境外的力量推动的——美国和欧洲以及其他地方的新市场对可卡因的持续巨大需求。

位于哥伦比亚首都波哥大的安第斯大学安全与毒品研究中心联席主任迈克尔·温特劳布（Michael Weintraub）表示：“目前需求不断扩大，因此会有供应来满足这一需求。” “这意味着哥伦比亚将在可预见的未来种植古柯。”

温特劳布还称哥伦比亚的安全环境“脆弱”——这使得替代计划很难在许多农村地区有效运作。

尽管 2016 年和平协议导致哥伦比亚革命武装力量大部分成员解散，但其他武装团体也已介入填补真空，并常常控制贩运路线和当地经济。

现任政府寻求通过“全面和平”政策来解决这个问题，即与所有不同的武装和犯罪团伙进行和平谈判。但分析人士表示，进展已经
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
